A sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is justified under 10 USC 2304(c)(5), FAR 6.302-5, authorized or required by statute.

FAR Part 19.1306(a) specify when a sole source HUBZone small business set-aside is appropriate. This acquisition meets those circumstances, as follows:
a. The contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns; Market research identified DH Technologies, LLC as the only HUBZone small business authorized distributor for these items;
b. The anticipated price of the contract, including options, will not exceed $6.5 million for a requirement within the NAICS codes for manufacturing or $4.5 million for a requirement, within any other NAICS code; the applicable NAICS code is 541519 so the $4.5 million limitation applies. As stated above, the estimated dollar value is $532,194 for the base year;
c. This is a new requirement that is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under the provisions of FAR subpart 19.8. No performance by an equal 8(a) participant was identified during market research;
d. The HUBZone small business concern has been determined to be a responsible contractor with respect to performance, DH Technologies, LLC has a history of successful performance on previous contracts for these items.

Example 3:
Market Research: In accordance with FAR Part 10, market research was conducted from 27 March 2025 to 08 April 2025. Market research on NASA SEWP identified eight (8) Service Women Owned Small Businesses (WOSBs) that are approved AWS resellers. Adequate price competition can be achieved for this requirement on NASA SEWP through WOSB competition. Therefore, it is the determination of the Contracting Officer to compete this requirement as a 100% WOSB set aside.

Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Example 2:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

Example 3:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

Example 1:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

To be considered for this work, the contractor must be capable of providing an MSP&A integration / innovation lab, supporting approximately 60 personnel, in the Colorado Springs, CO area that is approved (or will be within the current government fiscal year) to operate up to TS/SCI and SAP levels. The capabilities at this facility should allow multiple vendors to collaborate on analytical objectives supporting the customer(s).

2.0 Background
The Space Warfighting Analysis Center (SWAC) and the Space Security and Defense Program (SSDP) provide force design recommendations to the USSF via analytical studies, leveraging multiple vendors, government civilians, and military personnel.

The analysis conducted at the SWAC, SSDP, and other partner organizations informs strategic decisions on force structure and employment and are based on MSP&A utilizing models of varied fidelity. For the purposes of this RFI, the government is seeking solutions that primarily live in the mission-level MSP&A analytical area.

Example 3:
6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

The average total price of all quotes received for the base award was which was than the awardee's total quoted price of . The awardee was the lowest priced quoter with the fewest labor hours quoted, which means a different vendor would most likely be higher priced with more labor hours than the current vendor. The services are not separable and are intertwined and similar in nature with the amended PWS modification support not being materially different from the task order as awarded. Awarding the remaining aspects to a different vendor will create a high risk of contracting fratricide with separate vendors working overlapping aspects of the task order's PWS with no clear way to delineate lines of division. Separate task orders would ultimately slow down and degrade the effectiveness of the task order support. Therefore, logically separating the newly proposed support from the task order as awarded is not feasible nor is it in the government's best interests as it is more resource effective and efficient to maintain support through one vendor.

Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

3. SOLICITATION REQUIREMENTS, TERMS AND CONDITIONS
Contractors are required to meet all solicitation requirements. Quote validity period will be at least 180 calendar days from the closing date of this solicitation. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the Contractor being ineligible for award.

Normal duty tasks identified within this work statement shall be accomplished at the Naval Research Laboratory, BPTF located at 10050 Blossom Point Road, Welcome, Maryland, 20693. SECTION M (Evaluation Factors)

The award will be made based on the best value trade-off approach to the proposal that is determined to be the most beneficial to the Government, with appropriate consideration given to the 4 evaluation factors: Technical, Management Approach, Past Performance, and Price.

The Technical factor is significantly more important than the Management factor, which is more important than the Past Performance factor, which is more important than the Price factor. All non-priced Factors combined are more important than Price. Notwithstanding the importance of evaluation factors, Cost/Price may control the award decision when: (a) proposals are otherwise considered approximately equal in their non-cost/price factors, (b) an otherwise superior proposal is not offered at a fair and reasonable cost/price, or (c) the advantages of a higher rated, higher cost/price proposal are not considered to be worth the cost/price premium.

Offerors are cautioned that the award may not necessarily be made to the lowest price offered. When the lowest-priced proposal is rated highest on the other evaluation factors, it is the apparent best value. When the lowest-price proposal is not highest rated in the other evaluation factors, the Government shall determine whether the difference in price is worth the difference in the other factors. Award may be made to a technically superior, higher-priced proposal, when it is determined that the technical difference is sufficiently significant to outweigh the price difference. Conversely, an award may be made to a lower-priced proposal, lower technically rated proposal when it is determined that the cost premium involved in selecting a higher-priced proposal, higher rated proposal is not justified.

(1) To furnish the supplies or services set forth in the contract according to the specifications and the terms and conditions by subcontracting with the Offeror who has been determined an eligible concern pursuant to the provisions of section 8(a) of the Small Business Act, as amended (15 U.S.C.637(a)).
(2) Except for novation agreements, delegates to the 127 MSC the responsibility for administering the contract with complete authority to take any action on behalf of the Government under the terms and conditions of the contract; provided, however that the contracting agency shall give advance notice to the SBA before it issues a final notice terminating the right of the subcontractor to proceed with further performance, either in whole or in part, under the contract.
(3) That payments to be made under the contract will be made directly to the subcontractor by the contracting activity.
(4) To notify the 127 MSC. Contracting Officer immediately upon notification by the subcontractor that the owner or owners upon whom 8(a) eligibility was based plan to relinquish ownership or control of the concern.

In accordance with FAR 15.503(a)(2), a SPECIAL NOTICE providing preaward notice to small business offerors will be posted to the GPE (SAM.gov) today, 5 March 2026, prior to the award of contracts pursuant to the CASS acquisition, which is anticipated for Wednesday, 11 March 2026. When using a small business set-aside, as here (see FAR Subpart 19.5), "the contracting officer shall notify each offeror in writing prior to award . . . ." FAR 15.503(a)(2). This preaward notification for small businesses allows small business offerors an opportunity to protest an apparent successful offeror's small-business size representation prior to award of a contract or immediately thereafter. See FAR 19.302(d). A size protest filed prior to notice being made of the selection of the apparent successful offeror(s) would be dismissed as premature by the Small Business Administration (SBA). FAR 19.302(d)(4). Upon receipt of the SPECIAL NOTICE identifying the apparent successful offeror(s), any concern or other interested party may file a protest with the contracting officer; however, in order to be considered "timely" it must be received by the contracting officer by the close of business of the fifth business day after the SPECIAL NOTICE is received. See FAR 19.302(d)(1). A protest must be timely in order to affect a specific solicitation. FAR 19.302(d).

Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

Example 3:
7 Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately. Accuracy will be measured through both CMS and contractor tools that compare adjudication outcomes and artifacts with the legacy system.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Demonstrate the 276/277 X12 transaction flow is happening accurately and in near real-time.

RFP chunk to analyze:
BACKGROUND: Central Arkansas Veterans Healthcare System (CAVHS) has a requirement to upgrade the SPECTRALIS Software (HEYEX2).

SCOPE: HEYEX 2 is equipment that captures high-resolution, cross-sectional images of the retina and optic nerve, which allows providers to diagnose and treat retina and glaucoma-related diseases and conditions.

SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS:

Scalability: HEYEX 2 can scale from a small practice with a single device to a large enterprise with hundreds of imaging devices across multiple sites.

Database: It supports an SAP® SQL database for stability and scalability, which is essential for managing large volumes of data.

User Interface: HEYEX 2 features a robust clinical tool set that allows efficient data pre-processing and simultaneous access to patient records by multiple users.

Integration: The platform interfaces with Electronic Health Records (EHR) systems, eliminating duplicate entries and streamlining workflow.

Security: HEYEX 2 is designed with cybersecurity features to ensure the security of patient data and images.

TRAINING: The eye clinic already has this system at the stations; they just need to update the software and firmware.

Example 1:
PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.
Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

5.1.1 Web-Based Reporting System: The contractor shall support and maintain a web-based project tracking system for approximately 400 active SERDP and ESTCP projects. The incumbent contractor currently maintains the SERDP and ESTCP Management System (SEMS), which is used to track technical and financial status of projects, contract actions, document status, financial planning, and distribution. In addition, the contractor shall maintain information from past projects since FY95 within SEMS and analyze information as needed. The contractor shall adhere to security plans for SEMS according to industry best practices. The contractor shall work with the Defense Information Systems Agency (DISA) to ensure SEMS is in compliance with cybersecurity and other information management requirements. The contractor shall provide limited modifications as required by the Director to upgrade capability.

The Recreation Management Software must support industry open standard touch screen computers, bar code scanners, cash drawers, programmable keyboard or keypads, magnetic stripe readers, customer displays, mobile smart phones, tablets, and other peripheral hardware.

5.3.1 Provide a secure, cloud platform, housed in the continental United States with intuitive and user-friendly ESI data processing, review, analysis, predictive coding, artificial intelligence, and production tools. The software must be compatible with all browser applications including Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, and Safari.
5.3.2 Support multiple users simultaneously accessing the platform from secure, web-based connections.
5.3.3 The software functions cover the full cycle of Electronic Discovery Reference Model ("EDRM") including the following stages: Identification, Preservation, Collection, Processing, Review, Analysis, Production, and Presentation; essentially mapping out the entire process of managing electronically stored information (ESI) from initial identification to final production in a legal case.
5.3.3.1 Identification: Able to locate and identify potentially relevant ESI within the County's information ecosystem. Able to interface with County's Single Sign-On (SSO), Microsoft Office 365 and Active Directory.
5.3.3.2 Preservation: Able to place a legal hold on identified ESI to prevent its alteration or deletion. Able to send preservation notices and place custodians on legal holds using either vendor's legal holds component of the eDiscovery software or integrate with County's in-house Legal Holds Application. Initiate legal holds in Office 365 and track legal holds status.

Example 3:
The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

1.5. Place of Performance

Normal duty tasks identified within this work statement shall be accomplished at the Naval Research Laboratory, BPTF located at 10050 Blossom Point Road, Welcome, Maryland, 20693. Occasional travel may be required to government facilities or contractor facilities at remote sites both inside and outside the continental United States in order to perform ground command and control tasks as well as to maintain geographically separated remote antennas and associated infrastructure that are part of the BPTF baseline.

Due to the mission-critical nature of the work, security, operations, systems administration, and engineering functions are expected to maintain a daily onsite presence to ensure uninterrupted, real-time operational and mission support, technical integration, and compliance with operational and cybersecurity requirements.

The contractor shall submit all travel requirements (including plans, agenda, itinerary and dates) for pre-approval to the contracting officer's representative and is on a strictly cost reimbursable basis.

The contractor shall bill costs for travel in accordance with FAR 31.205-46 Travel Costs (subject to local policy & procedures).

Example 3:
Key Roles

Listed are the key roles the contractor shall provide at time of contract award and the general functionality of their position is listed in Appendix G. The minimum education, training, and experience required by contractor personnel (whether prime or subcontractor) to perform support tasks identified in this contract are defined in the labor category descriptions provided herein.

Additional information on these categories is found in Appendix G. Personnel in labor categories designated as key roles cannot be replaced without the approval of the government. The following are identified as key roles:

Program Manager
Systems Engineer
Facilities Manager
Security Manager

Beyond these key roles, non-key personnel categories include: Telecommunications Engineer, Cybersecurity Engineer, Systems Administrator, Information System Security Engineer (ISSE), Administrative Assistant, CAD Technician, Radio Frequency Systems Technologist, Information System Security Officer (ISSO), Logistician, Business Process Manager, Purchasing and Materials Coordinator, Project Manager, Configuration Manager, Information Technology Architect, Antenna Specialist, and Software Developer.

Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

DISPUTES - Except as otherwise provided in this contract, when a dispute arises between the parties and it cannot be resolved by direct negotiation, either party may request a dispute hearing with Agent. The request for a dispute hearing must: be in writing, state the disputed issue(s), state the relative positions of the parties, state the Contractor's name, address, and contract number; and be mailed to the Agent and the other party's (respondent's) Contract Manager within 3 working days after the parties agree that they cannot resolve the dispute. The respondent shall send a written answer to the requester's statement to both the agent and the requester within 5 working days. The Agent shall review the written statements and reply in writing to both parties within 10 working days. The Agent may extend this period, if necessary, by notifying the parties. The parties agree that this dispute process shall precede any action in a judicial or quasi-judicial tribunal. Nothing in this Contract shall be construed to limit the parties' choice of a mutually acceptable ADR method in addition to the dispute resolution procedure outlined above.

RFP chunk to analyze:
The USCG intends to procure brand name DataSwitch & Maestro ICAN software IPs in order to support the NAIS program. The USCG has determined ICAN currently holds all intellectual rights to the Data Switch and Maestro IPs and only its authorized service providers are capable of providing the necessary software maintenance support. As there are no authorized resellers, this will be a sole source to ICAN. ICAN, Inc. is the only source capable of providing the required products and services, as there are no authorized resellers for ICAN systems or software. The ICAN system operates on proprietary software, developed and owned by ICAN, Inc. This contract requires ongoing software maintenance and upgrades, which requires access to the system's source code for its core components, the "Data Switch" and "Maestro" applications. Without these updates, the software may not function as intended, potentially resulting in lost data, system outages, and other service interruptions. The Government does not possess the intellectual property rights for this software. As a result, it is legally impossible to provide the source code to any other contractor, precluding third-party maintenance or upgrades. Market Research, as documented in Section 8, confirmed this limitation. Four (4) companies expressed interest in performing the work; however, all were found technically unacceptable solely because they could not obtain access to the necessary proprietary code. ICAN, Inc. has not authorized any resellers to provide their software. Alternative software solutions cannot be considered at this time due to specific requirements for data handling, such as National Marine Electronics Association (NMEA) protocols, real-time testing, and backend data parsing and archival system requirements.

Requirements – Technical: The selected software must meet the following criteria: Content Management System – preference will be given to a mature, proven well-known CMS that supports multiple secure user accounts that enable specific view/report/add/edit permissions. Software should be reliable, secure and robust, in accordance with industry standards and practices. The software must be cloud based (software as a service) platform. Ability to integrate with other software solutions via an API or other methods that would allow the potential for CRM and Web Site interfaces. Software will contain an accurate and complete audit trail that ensures efficient processing. Software must be able to identify and itemize all fees; such as program, rental, transaction, and convenience fees. Must have the ability to provide total program costs to client. Software must be compatible with the County's financial software, Tyler Munis, and post transactions to the General Ledger. Software shall include the ability to implement different security access levels, and elevated admin privileges to appropriate staff. Launch Live Date – Targeted for July 1, 2026. Software must have a comprehensive storage procedure to store all documents and records in a manner that is easily retrievable by County. Software must have cybersecurity protections and company must provide cyberliability insurance coverage.

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Contracting Officer will determine that the anticipated cost to the Government will be fair and reasonable based on the IGCE, prior acquisition history, and ICAN's available pricing for the same/similar products. The USCG conducted market research on SAM.gov releasing a Request for Information (RFI) on 03 October 2025 and closing on 17 October 2025. Of the four (4) responses, none of the vendors were authorized resellers of the ICAN Software and Licenses and therefore, were not capable of the requirements solicited. Therefore, based on market research, it has been determined that no other source possesses the technical qualifications, proprietary resources, and required legal access to the ICAN source code. As such, ICAN, Inc. is the only responsible source capable of performing this work, and no other supplies or services will satisfy the agency's minimum requirements. It is anticipated that this brand name software will be required for the foreseeable future in a sole source capacity. However, the Government will continue to conduct market research into other software and support options that could potentially satisfy future requirements or determine if authorized resellers are ever approved.
